title: Decoding the Dual Reign: Navigating the Coexistence of Income Tax Act, 2025 and Income Tax Act, 1961
content:
# Decoding the Dual Reign: Navigating the Coexistence of Income Tax Act, 2025 and Income Tax Act, 1961

Hello fellow professionals,

The Indian tax landscape has been abuzz since April 1, 2026, when the much-anticipated **Income Tax Act, 2025** (hereafter, "New Act") officially came into effect, aiming to simplify and modernize our direct tax system. Replacing the venerable, six-decade-old **Income Tax Act, 1961** (hereafter, "Old Act"), this is undoubtedly a monumental shift. However, if you thought the Old Act would simply vanish overnight, *toh aap galat hain*. The reality, as recently clarified by the Income Tax Department, is a nuanced period of coexistence and careful transition.

Many of us are scratching our heads, wondering, "Does the old law apply, or the new one? What about my pending assessments? Mere carry-forward losses ka kya hoga?" This is precisely why the Income Tax Department's comprehensive clarifications on the interplay and transitional provisions are a breath of fresh air. They act as a critical roadmap for taxpayers and practitioners navigating this dual legal universe.

## Why the Dual Regime? The Inevitable Overlap

The Income Tax Act, 2025, while a complete replacement, doesn't erase the past. Tax laws, as we know, don't operate strictly within annual boundaries. While new income and transactions from **Tax Year 2026-27** onwards fall under the purview of the New Act, income earned up to March 31, 2026, continues to be governed by the Old Act.

This necessitates a **transitional phase** where both legislations will run in parallel for several years. The primary reasons for this intricate overlap are administrative and legal continuity:

*   **Pending Assessments and Litigation:** Think about all those scrutiny assessments, reassessments, appeals, and other disputes initiated under the 1961 Act. These cannot simply be abandoned. They will continue to be governed by the Old Act until their final resolution.
*   **Accrued Rights and Liabilities:** Any rights (like refunds) or liabilities (like outstanding tax demands) that accrued under the Old Act will remain valid and enforceable, even after the New Act's commencement.
*   **Historical Context:** The 1961 Act had accumulated numerous amendments, provisos, and explanations over six decades, making it quite complex. The New Act aims to present the same tax policy in a more logical and readable format, but this simplification doesn't mean a complete overhaul of underlying principles.

## Decoding the Transitional Provisions: Section 536 – The Legal Bridge

The cornerstone of this transition is **Section 536** of the New Act, often referred to as the "Repeal and Savings" clause. This section acts as the crucial legal bridge, formally repealing the 1961 Act while simultaneously **saving** everything that was earned, accrued, pending, or initiated under it. The Income Tax Department's clarification delves into various facets of this saving clause, ensuring no taxpayer is left in a legal void.

Let's look at some key areas where this clarification provides much-needed guidance:

### 1. Applicability Based on Tax Year

This is perhaps the most fundamental point.
*   **Income up to March 31, 2026:** All income earned, accrued, or arising during the financial years ending on or before March 31, 2026, will be assessed and governed by the provisions of the **Income Tax Act, 1961**. This includes related compliance, assessments, and appeals.
*   **Income from April 1, 2026, onwards:** Any income accruing or arising on or after April 1, 2026, will be subject to the **Income Tax Act, 2025**.

### 2. The "Tax Year" Revolution vs. Old Assessment Year

One of the most significant structural changes introduced by the New Act is the replacement of the dual concepts of "Previous Year" and "Assessment Year" with a single, unified "**Tax Year**" (April 1 to March 31). While this simplifies compliance going forward, it's crucial to remember that the "Assessment Year" concept remains highly relevant for all matters pertaining to the periods governed by the Old Act.

### 3. Continuity of Proceedings

The clarifications emphasize that the transition is not meant to disrupt ongoing legal processes.
*   **Pending Assessments and Appeals:** Any assessment, reassessment, appeal, rectification, or revision proceedings initiated under the 1961 Act for tax years prior to **Tax Year 2026-27** will continue to be governed by the provisions of the **1961 Act**, even if these proceedings are finalized after April 1, 2026.
*   **Remand Proceedings:** If an appellate authority remands a case back to a lower authority for a period before April 1, 2026, those remand proceedings will also continue under the Old Act.

### 4. Carry Forward of Losses and Tax Credits

This is an area where taxpayers often face anxiety during legislative changes. The good news is that the New Act, through its transitional provisions, ensures continuity.
*   **Accumulated Losses:** Losses determined for tax years prior to April 1, 2026, under the 1961 Act can be carried forward and set off against income computed under the New Act. The manner and period for carrying forward these losses will generally remain as provided under the repealed 1961 Act.
*   **MAT/AMT Credit:** Similarly, Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT) or Alternate Minimum Tax (AMT) credits accumulated under the Old Act will be deemed as credit under the corresponding provisions of the New Act and can be utilized for the balance period, subject to prescribed conditions.

### 5. Tax Payments, Refunds, and Compliance Mechanisms

Core compliance mechanisms remain largely consistent, though the governing law depends on the year of income:
*   **Advance Tax/TDS/TCS:** While the *policy* for these remains, the specific forms and procedural requirements for income earned from April 1, 2026, will align with the **Income Tax Rules, 2026**, framed under the New Act. For income years before this, the old rules apply.
*   **Refunds and Demands:** Any entitlement to a refund or an outstanding tax demand under the 1961 Act remains valid and can be claimed or recovered, respectively, even after the New Act comes into force.

## Old Provision vs. New Provision: A Conceptual Look

While the new Act aims at simplification rather than a complete overhaul of tax rates or policy, certain structural and definitional changes will impact application. For instance, the elimination of alphabets in section numbers or the clearer drafting of certain provisions means that while the spirit might be similar, the letter of the law has changed.

| Aspect                       | Under Income Tax Act, 1961 (for past years)                          | Under Income Tax Act, 2025 (from FY 2026-27 onwards)                 |
| :--------------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Taxable Period Terminology** | Previous Year & Assessment Year                                      | Single "Tax Year" (April 1 to March 31)            |
| **Governing Law for Income** | Income earned up to March 31, 2026                                   | Income earned from April 1, 2026                |
| **Pending Assessments**      | Governed by 1961 Act until completion     | New assessments for new income governed by 2025 Act                  |
| **Carry Forward of Losses**  | As per 1961 Act principles, but set off under 2025 Act | As per 2025 Act principles                                           |
| **Act Structure**            | ~819 sections, often with many sub-sections and provisos   | ~536 sections, simplified drafting, rationalized structure |

## Final Thoughts: Adaptability is Key

The transition to the Income Tax Act, 2025, while designed for simplification, presents its own set of complexities during this interim period. The Income Tax Department's clarifications, especially the comprehensive FAQs, are invaluable resources for understanding **where the old law applies, and where the new one kicks in**.

As tax professionals, our role becomes even more critical. We must be masters of both regimes for the foreseeable future, meticulously tracking the assessment year, the nature of the transaction, and the specific proceeding to determine the correct applicable law. *Yeh naya phase hai, aur isme dhyan rakhna hoga!* The emphasis is on precise year-wise application and careful documentation to avoid potential disputes.

Embrace these clarifications, stay updated with further guidance from the CBDT, and ensure your systems and understanding are aligned with this evolving legal landscape. This era demands vigilance, adaptability, and an in-depth understanding of the intricate dance between the old and the new.

* [Visit the Income Tax Department's Official Portal for Acts, Rules & Notifications](https://incometax.gov.in/iec/foportal/)

*Published on: 28 June 2026*
